title: ImageNet: The Giant Library
concept: ImageNet is a massive dataset of over 14 million images that revolutionized deep learning and object classification.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why must the testing set remain hidden during model training?

options.text: To prevent the model from memorizing the test questions and overestimating its accuracy
To minimize the dataset storage footprint on the hard drive
To avoid running the convolutional layers in reverse

exercises.explain: Hiding the test set prevents data leakage and ensures we measure true generalization to unseen data.

cards.title: The Spark of Modern AI
cards.body: In 2012, a model named AlexNet achieved a breakthrough in image recognition, crushing traditional computer vision methods. This watershed moment was only possible because of ImageNet, a project that took years to organize and clean.

cards.title: ImageNet
cards.body: ImageNet is a database of over 14 million annotated images organized according to the WordNet hierarchy. The annual ILSVRC challenge was the premier benchmark for classification models.

cards.title: An Encyclopedia with Pictures
cards.body: Imagine a giant encyclopedia containing thousands of concepts. For each word (like 'dalmatian' or 'firetruck'), ImageNet provides hundreds of real-world photos illustrating it, mapping abstract language to raw pixels.

cards.title: The 1000-Class Challenge
cards.body: The standard ImageNet benchmark (ILSVRC) uses a subset of 1,000 classes. Models are trained on 1.2 million images and evaluated on their top-1 or top-5 prediction error rate on a separate test set.

cards.title: Not a modern perfect standard
cards.body: You might think ImageNet is perfect. In reality, it has historical biases, duplicates, and labels that are ambiguous (e.g., distinguishing between very similar sub-species of dogs that even humans confuse).

left.text:
9,000 images total.
101 classes.
Easy to overfit, poor generalization.

right.text:
14 million+ images.
20,000+ classes.
Required deep neural networks to learn.

exercises.prompt: Which architecture's breakthrough on ImageNet in 2012 sparked the modern deep learning boom?
exercises.explain: AlexNet's massive win in the 2012 ImageNet challenge proved the power of deep convolutional networks.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The standard ImageNet classification challenge (ILSVRC) contains over 10 million distinct classes.
exercises.explain: False. While ImageNet has over 20,000 classes overall, the standard ILSVRC challenge used a subset of 1,000 classes.
WordNet is the lexical database of English words that maps concepts hierarchically in ImageNet.
exercises.sentence: ImageNet images are structured using a word hierarchy called ___.

exercises.prompt: Why was ImageNet crucial for the development of deep neural networks?
exercises.explain: Deep models have millions of parameters; they require massive datasets like ImageNet to generalize effectively.

options.text: Its massive size provided enough training examples to feed deep networks without immediate overfitting
It was the first dataset to store images in compressed ZIP format
It contained code that automatically computed gradients on GPUs

exercises.prompt: What is the key difference between top-1 error rate and top-5 error rate in the ImageNet benchmark?

options.text: Top-1 counts a prediction as wrong if the single highest-probability class is incorrect; top-5 counts it as correct if the true class is in the model's top 5 highest-probability guesses.
Top-1 is evaluated on the training set; top-5 is evaluated on the validation set.
Top-1 measures classification accuracy; top-5 measures bounding box overlap.
Top-5 is five times slower to calculate than top-1.

exercises.explain: Top-5 error rate is a more lenient metric, commonly used when objects in an image might be ambiguous or contain multiple classes.
